questions.question: Einstein's special relativity is based on two postulates. One is that the speed of light is constant. The other is:
options: The laws of physics are the same in all inertial reference frames
questions.explanation: Two postulates: (1) Laws of physics are identical in all inertial frames (no preferred frame). (2) Speed of light (c) is the same for all observers regardless of their motion. From these, time dilation, length contraction, and E=mc² follow.

questions.question: According to special relativity, a clock moving relative to you appears to tick SLOWER than your own clock.
questions.explanation: Time dilation: moving clocks run slow. t' = t/√(1-v²/c²). At 0.87c: time passes at half rate. GPS satellites (14,000 km/h) lose 7μs/day due to special relativistic time dilation. This is experimentally confirmed with muons and atomic clocks on planes.

questions.question: Einstein's famous equation E = mc² shows that mass and ___ are equivalent.
questions.explanation: E=mc²: a small amount of mass contains enormous energy (c² ≈ 9×10¹⁶ m²/s²). 1kg of matter fully converted = 9×10¹⁶ joules = ~21 megatons TNT. This powers nuclear reactions (fission and fusion) where a tiny mass deficit becomes energy.

questions.question: A spaceship travels at 0.8c relative to Earth. If 10 years pass on Earth, how much time passes on the spaceship?
questions.explanation: Lorentz factor γ = 1/√(1-0.8²) = 1/√0.36 = 1/0.6 = 1.667. Ship time = Earth time / γ = 10/1.667 = 6 years. The ship's crew ages 6 years while Earth ages 10. This is the 'twin paradox' (not actually paradoxical — the traveler accelerated).

questions.question: GPS satellites need both special and general relativity corrections. Without them, positioning errors would accumulate at ~10 km/day. Why do BOTH relativistic effects matter?

scenario.context: GPS: 24 satellites at 20,200 km altitude, orbiting at 3.9 km/s. Atomic clocks onboard must match ground clocks to within nanoseconds.

effects.General relativity: Clocks in weaker gravity (higher altitude) run faster → +45 μs/day

options: Special only — satellites move fast, causing time dilation. General relativity is negligible for GPS.
General only — altitude gravity difference dominates. Speed effect is negligible.
Both — special (speed → slow clocks: -7μs/day) and general (weaker gravity → fast clocks: +45μs/day). Net: +38μs/day.
Neither — GPS uses signal propagation timing, not relativistic corrections.
questions.explanation: Both effects are measurable: SR slows satellite clocks by 7μs/day, GR speeds them by 45μs/day. Net: satellites gain 38μs/day vs ground. Without correction: c × 38μs ≈ 11.4 km/day positioning error. GPS firmware pre-adjusts the satellite clock frequency.

questions.question: If interstellar travel at relativistic speeds becomes possible, the 'twin paradox' becomes real: travelers age less than those who stay. Should travelers be able to return to a future Earth and claim their property/assets?
questions.scenario: A traveler leaves at 0.99c for a 10-year journey (ship time). On Earth, 70 years pass. Their family has aged/died. Their property was distributed. Their investments grew for 70 years. Society has transformed.

options.label: Yes — they're the same legal person. Time dilation doesn't void ownership
No — after a threshold (30 years?), absent persons are legally 'dead' (similar to current missing persons law)
options.framework: Practicality — society can't preserve indefinite claims

options.label: Time-trust: assets go into trust for potential return, managed by AI fiduciary for up to 200 years
options.framework: Institutional solution — new legal structures for new physics

options.label: New framework needed — relativistic travel creates new legal categories (not alive, not dead)
options.framework: Legal innovation — physics requires new law

questions.explanation: Time-trusts (already discussed in space law literature) would preserve assets and legal identity for relativistic travelers. Similar to existing trusts that span generations, but with the beneficiary being their future self. Requires new legal frameworks as interstellar travel becomes feasible.
